title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
subtitle: This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.
caption: From 1957 to 2026, the cumulative number of objects launched into Earth orbit or beyond has increased from zero to 25,000 globally. The chart tracks this accumulation across multiple nations and agencies, with the World total line rising steadily through the mid-20th century before accelerating sharply after 2010. The United States line follows a similar trajectory but at a lower magnitude, reaching approximately 15,000 by 2026. Russia’s line climbs more gradually, plateauing near 4,000 after 2000. China’s line remains flat until the 1990s, then rises steeply to surpass 2,000 by 2026. Japan, France, India, and Germany each show modest growth, with lines hovering between 500 and 1,500 objects. The European Space Agency line begins in the 1980s and reaches just under 1,000 by 2026. The United Kingdom line stays near zero until the 2010s, then climbs slightly above 500. All national lines remain below the World total, which aggregates all launches regardless of origin. The steepest rise occurs between 2020 and 2026, where the World line gains over 10,000 objects — more than double the gain of the previous decade. The United States line also accelerates during this period, gaining roughly 7,000 objects. No other country or agency shows comparable growth in this final segment. The data shows that launch activity has concentrated increasingly in the most recent years, with the global total doubling since 2015.
